title: Protection of Women and Girls in Sports Act of 2025
short_title: Protection of Women and Girls in Sports Act of 2025
stage_label: Ms. Adams moved to recommit to the Committee on Education and Workforce. (text: CR H137)
plain_summary: This bill changes the rules for school sports teams that get money from the government. It says that people who were born male cannot play on girls' or women's sports teams. The bill decides if someone is male or female based on how they were born, not how they identify now. Males can still practice with girls' teams as long as it doesn't take away chances from female players. The government will also study how having males on girls' teams might hurt female athletes.
policy_area: Civil Rights and Liberties, Minority Issues

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Protection of Women and Girls in Sports Act of two thousand twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2  Amendment
sections.text: This section changes the Education Amendments of nineteen seventy two by adding new rules. These rules say that schools and organizations that receive federal money cannot allow a person whose sex is male to participate in sports programs or activities that are meant for women or girls. For these rules, a person's sex is determined only by their reproductive biology and genetics at the time they were born. Athletic programs and activities include all programs or activities that require being part of any sports team. However, males can train or practice with women's or girls' sports teams as long as no female loses her spot on the team, loses chances to practice or compete, loses a scholarship, loses admission to school, or loses any other benefit that comes from being part of the sports program. The Comptroller General (a government official who reviews spending and programs) must do a study to figure out what other benefits means in this context. The study will look at benefits that women and girls get from playing in single sex sports that would be lost if males were allowed to participate. The study must record the harmful psychological, developmental, participatory, and sociological effects on girls when males are allowed to compete, be team members, or participate in sports programs designed for girls. This includes being displaced or discouraged from sports participation, losing team spots, losing practice or competition opportunities, losing scholarships or scholarship chances, losing or being displaced from school admission, losing the benefit of an environment free from hostility based on sexual assault or harassment, or losing any other benefit that comes with participating in the sports program. The Comptroller General must send a report with the study results to the Committee on Education and the Workforce of the House of Representatives and the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions of the Senate.
